text: It's hard to articulate, but I'll try.  :slightly_smiling_face:  For me, Glitch's brand is friendly but the visuals are still just graphical and decorative.  Storyscript feels more illustrative, and the Inkie character is very prominent -- there is a bigger gulf between storybook illustrations and the tech language being used.  For instance, in the book, you jump from a story for 5-year-olds ,to phrases like "Automated infrastructure configuration, scheduling and management."  It's like creating a power tool with Fisher Price aesthetics -- the tool might be amazing, but there is a dissonance there.  Which is different from making a power tool with colorful ergonomic rubber handles and a plain English user manual.  Still friendly but more in line with the target audience.  Just my opinion though.  Hope that helps!

I think if you can build something with a tool that gets people 200K-500K salaries (or more) if they learn how to us it effectively then it will become the "one thing" programmers use over time, even if that thing looked like a toy at first, the trick is to get there you need to get hobbyists, kids and maybe entrepreneurs to adopt it.  Another option is to dictate that its the "official" option for an important platform or environment.

I would like to caution you that Reddit has many inhabitants whose chief delight is crapping all over any proposed changes to the status quo. I recommend before you jump into the swamp that is called Reddit, you watch a small fragment of Carroll Shelby's interview, where he talks about dealing with the bureaucracy inside Ford.  See this video at 29:30 <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rzq4DeTjZ1A&t=4892s> where he talks about how hard it was to work with Ford. He managed to run the most expensive skunkworks group the auto industry has ever seen, and any creative person has to admire the determination and salesmanship of Shelby. A movie portraying the massive effort by Ford to beat Ferrari opens tomorrow, and i will be there early! Software isn't like cars, but big companies are the same everywhere!

I'd like to reinforce <@U8A5MS6R1>'s point because so many here seem to overlook it. Plain text is so extremely successful because it has inherited thousands of years of writing technology. And it's used for so much more than just programming.

Hypothesis: we'll see a surge in semantic editing when it becomes available for plain language as well. Word processors could offer refactoring ("remove passive voice") as much as software editors. And then we'd start to consider it normal.

The problem is that “plain text” isn’t that old - and it’s more of a problem outside the English-speaking world. What exactly is plain anyway? No accents? Which subset of math symbols and punctuation will do?

Hey! I'm Piotr. I've found FoC by googling for non-coding services.

I've created a service for building machine learning models without coding: <https://mljar.com>

I'm not happy with my service. It's only building ML models. Users need to do a lot of additional work to use the ML model in production. And I think that knowing how to use ML models is maybe too complex.

I would like to extend/change the mljar. I would like to let users do data analysis (dashboards and apps). I've started looking at different non-coding platforms for data science. This is how I've found this community :slightly_smiling_face:

attachments.title: Machine Learning for Humans
attachments.text: Automated Machine Learning platform. With MLJAR anyone can train great machine learning models!

<@UK22Z72L8> I believe Storyscript, and products like it, will be a driving force for the next generation of developers. Today we have 50 million developers… in 10 years there will be many, many more. I believe an order of magnitude more. Historically speaking this was the case 3 times: introduction of assembly, then low-level, then high-level languages. Each time an order of magnitude change that multiplied the workforce. This will happen again.

Hmm, I guess I might have the wrong thing on my mind then, apologies for misleading, I’m not really good at that level of math and might have had something wrong while researching :sweat_smile:! What I’m calling a lattice is having graphs/sets A,B,C..n in which elements of B can be elements of A but with a transformation applied. Like having a graph A and graph B and node X from B is actually X from A with a transformation applied and different relationships.

So my lattice structure is a structure containing all those nodes and their relationships in each set (edges) they’re a member of or connected to.  Basically an array of graphs where each node can exist in multiple graphs and have different relationships in each, but it’s always the same node just with a transformation applied for that graph type.  So if you have an X in set D, you can always get relationships of X in set A.

Here in London at the extreme tuesday club,  somebody asked a brilliant question: “I am a professional project manager that has worked in many industries. I’m going to be managing my first software development team. What do I need to learn/know?” - I wish more people asked that. I’d say “The mythical man-month” and “Peopleware” would be to old-but-good books.

Thanks for clarifying, <@UNBPP291C>! It doesn’t sound dumb at all.

What is your motivation? Are you trying to stay flexible with the data model and/or does it change frequently?

Are your sets (which I understand model the edges in a graph?) small, just modeling relationships between few nodes, but then you deal with a large number of edges?

Theoretically, you could model everything with just binary relationships and take advantage of all the standards graph algorithms, but there are obvious benefits of being able to attach metadata to those edges and factor out some of your data model into such metadata. That would put you into classic graph database territory. Have you considered using a graph database and if so what made you decide against it?

If your sets for modeling relationships have more than two nodes, do they all have the same number or does it vary? In that case, or maybe in general, a more specific example with perhaps actual data could be enlightening.

And I guess the most interesting question is: you asked if somebody here has played with this — I’m still not sure I did, because I’m still not sure what _this_ is, but… what other questions do you have?

I’m a programmer and I use spreadsheets for accounting tasks. Do other programmers not do this? Likewise, I use diagramming and image editing apps, although I suppose I could write code to do those things.

I’m a programmer and I use spreadsheets for accounting tasks. Do other programmers not do this? Likewise, I use diagramming and image editing apps, although I suppose I could write code to do those things.

'Text' is very broad. I try to distinguish 'plain text file based programming' from 'text in programming'. Text is programming seems fine - you want to write formulae, queries, labels, names, other snippets etc. But consider how spreadsheets, subtext, notebooks/observablehq, smalltalk etc don't just fit into the mainstream software dev ecosystem even though they feature plenty of text.

'Text' is very broad. I try to distinguish 'plain text file based programming' from 'text in programming'. Text is programming seems fine - you want to write formulae, queries, labels, names, other snippets etc. But consider how spreadsheets, subtext, notebooks/observablehq, smalltalk etc don't just fit into the mainstream software dev ecosystem even though they feature plenty of text.

I've recently been thinking in terms of 'correspondence' and find it useful to think about the strength of models. E.g. plain text has a very strong correspondence between the display model and storage model (linear, 1-to-1, with some special markers like newlines). Nothing is hidden. It also has a strong correspondence with the input method: keyboard, often 1 key -> 1 char (but not always). If I see a picture of some code anywhere, I know instantly how to recreate it 'precisely'. Can't do that with a vector diagram or a spreadsheet. Also the standard storage operations (read/write) give me exactly the same elements (chars) that correspond directly with ~keys and visual cells. This is the appeal of plain text files.

Where it falls short is when I seek stronger correspondence to other models represented in the plain text (e.g. AST or some other structure, inter links, etc.). Now there's parsing and file formats involved, a lot of duplication of logic across different readers that interpret the text, false versioniong (text versioning != ast versioning) and so on.
